description: Student consistently uses addition/subtraction to solve proportion and ratio problems instead of multiplicative reasoning. This is the most fundamental barrier to proportional understanding.
subject: ratios_proportions
diagnosis: Student has not made the transition from additive to multiplicative reasoning. All ratio and proportion work is built on this foundation. Without intervention, this will block progress in percentage, algebra, and science.
symptoms: Adds the same number to both quantities when scaling
Compares ratios by subtracting rather than dividing
Correct on easy problems (direct doubling) but fails on non-integer scales
intervention_focus: Build multiplicative comparison with concrete materials before returning to abstract ratio notation

description: Student can mechanically simplify or compute ratios but applies the wrong procedure in unfamiliar contexts, revealing lack of conceptual understanding of what a ratio represents.
subject: ratios_proportions
diagnosis: Student has memorized some procedures (cross-multiply, divide) but doesn't understand the underlying proportional relationships. Knowledge is brittle and fails when context changes.
symptoms: Incorrect on word problems requiring ratio interpretation
intervention_focus: Re-teach what a ratio means using real objects and contexts before procedural practice

description: Student confuses ratios (part-to-part) with fractions (part-to-whole), leading to systematic errors when converting between the two or dividing quantities in a given ratio.
subject: ratios_proportions
diagnosis: Student cannot reliably distinguish between 'the ratio of A to B is 1:3' and 'A is 1/3 of the total'. This confusion is extremely common and affects percentage, probability, and statistics.
symptoms: Given ratio a:b, computes a/b instead of a/(a+b) for fraction of whole
In profit-sharing problems, treats ratio directly as fraction of total profit
Finds total mixture instead of one component
intervention_focus: Explicit side-by-side comparison of part-part and part-whole representations using diagrams

description: Student struggles specifically with unit rate problems — either skipping the normalization step or applying the wrong operation when finding cost/speed/rate per single unit.
subject: ratios_proportions
diagnosis: Student understands proportional scaling in simple cases but breaks down when required to find a per-unit rate before scaling. The two-step process (divide then multiply) is not consolidated.
symptoms: Multiplies directly by the new quantity without dividing by original quantity
Makes arithmetic errors in the division step
Correct when scale factor is a whole number, wrong when it requires division
intervention_focus: Explicit two-step rate problems with emphasis on the 'per unit' concept

description: Student cannot distinguish between direct and inverse proportional relationships and defaults to direct proportion in all contexts.
subject: ratios_proportions
diagnosis: Student has only one mental model of proportion (more of A → more of B) and cannot recognize when increasing one quantity should decrease another. Affects speed-time, work-time, and resource allocation problems.
symptoms: Halves the days when workers double instead of halving days
Sets up inverse proportion as direct proportion
Cannot identify which situations are inversely proportional
intervention_focus: Contrasting direct and inverse proportion with real-world investigations (e.g., more workers vs fewer days)
